stimuli.description: 1-back trial where the current letter matches the letter shown 1 trial ago (case-insensitive).
1-back trial where the current letter does NOT match the letter shown 1 trial ago.
2-back trial where the current letter matches the letter shown 2 trials ago (case-insensitive).
2-back trial where the current letter does NOT match the letter shown 2 trials ago.

phase_detection.feedback: (function(){var el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus');if(!el)return false;if(el.querySelector('img'))return false;var t=el.innerHTML.toLowerCase();return t.indexOf('press enter')!==-1 || t.indexOf('press enter')!==-1;})()

task_specific.key_mapping_notes: Response key for match vs mismatch is counterbalanced across participants via window.efVars.group_index. For group_index 0-4: match='.', mismatch=','. For group_index 5-14: match=',', mismatch='.'. The experiment writes the per-trial correct key into the global var 'correctResponse' when each trial's stimulus is generated, so the bot reads window.correctResponse to determine the correct response for the current trial without needing to track group_index, letter history, or delay in JS.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian decomposition of n-back / updating RTs in young adults yielded mu values typically in the 400-560 ms range.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Mean RTs on 1-back letter trials averaged ~600 ms in healthy young adults, with target (match) RTs slightly slower than non-target RTs.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for updating-task ex-Gaussian fits clustered around 40-90 ms in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Tau, the exponential tail component, ranged from approximately 80-180 ms across updating sub-tasks, with longer tails on harder load conditions.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task.
citations.quote: Tau commonly absorbs 100-200 ms of slow-tail variance in healthy-adult speeded-choice tasks.

match_1back.rationale: Healthy-adult n-back RT distributions are well fit by ex-Gaussian (Schmiedek et al., 2007). 1-back match trials show a moderately positively skewed RT around 640-660 ms; mu set near the modal RT with tau capturing the slower-target tail.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Non-target (mismatch) trials in 1-back letter tasks were faster than match trials, with means in the upper-500 to mid-600 ms range.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Mu for non-target updating responses tended to be ~10-30 ms below target-trial mu in matched contrasts.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Non-target (mismatch) trials showed slightly tighter sigma than target trials in updating tasks.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Tau on non-target updating trials clustered around 70-160 ms, generally below target-trial tau.

mismatch_1back.rationale: Mismatch (non-target) 1-back trials are slightly faster than match trials in healthy adults, with a tighter tail because the default 'no match' response can be released without explicit target verification.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: 2-back letter target RTs were ~80-150 ms slower than 1-back target RTs in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: Mean RTs on the 2-back single condition averaged in the 600-720 ms range across young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Sigma increased modestly with WM-load demands; high-load updating trials showed sigma roughly 50-100 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Tau is the component most sensitive to working-memory load; high-load trials produced tau in the 130-230 ms range.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O.
citations.title: Individual differences in working memory and processing speed.
citations.quote: Increases in n-back load primarily inflate the tau (slow-tail) component of RT distributions.

match_2back.rationale: Increasing N from 1 to 2 inflates both mu and tau in healthy adults (Schmiedek et al., 2007; Kane et al., 2007). Match trials require successful retrieval of the lag-2 item; the longer tau reflects occasional retrieval failures that resolve slowly.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Non-target 2-back RTs were faster than target 2-back RTs by ~30-60 ms in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Sigma on 2-back non-target trials approximated 40-95 ms in young-adult fits.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Non-target 2-back tau values were typically 100-200 ms, somewhat below target-trial tau but above 1-back equivalents.

mismatch_2back.rationale: 2-back mismatch trials are faster than 2-back match trials but slower than any 1-back condition. Tau is elevated relative to 1-back to reflect maintenance demands across the lag-2 buffer.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f noise.
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations of trial RTs in choice tasks generally fall between ~0.1 and ~0.3 in healthy adult samples.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise.
citations.quote: Trial-by-trial RT series in cognitive tasks display short-lag positive autocorrelation consistent with low-order AR processes (phi ~0.1-0.3) embedded in long-range structure.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation around phi ~0.2 is a robust feature of speeded-choice and working-memory tasks (Wagenmakers, Farrell, & Ratcliff, 2004). Enabled for n-back.

citations.authors: Lim, J., Wu, W.-C., Wang, J., Detre, J. A., Dinges, D. F., & Rao, H.
citations.title: Imaging brain fatigue from sustained mental workload: An ASL perfusion study of the time-on-task effect.
citations.quote: Mean RT increased monotonically with time-on-task across a 20-min sustained-WM block, on the order of tens of ms over hundreds of trials.

citations.authors: Sanders, A. F.
citations.title: Issues and trends in the debate on discrete vs. continuous processing of information.
citations.quote: Time-on-task RT slowing in choice tasks is typically a fraction of a millisecond per trial in well-practiced young adults.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Mild positive RT drift across blocks is documented in extended n-back sessions, attributed to vigilance decrement under sustained WM load (Lim et al., 2010). Conservative ~0.25 ms/trial keeps cumulative drift modest within a typical session.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: N-back RT analyses do not document a stable condition-repetition (match->match or mismatch->mismatch) facilitation effect separable from the basic match/mismatch contrast.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: No reliable condition-repetition cost in n-back is documented in this validation study.

condition_repetition.rationale: Repetition priming for stimulus letters is partly reflected in match vs mismatch by design; literature does not document a separable condition-repetition cost beyond the match/mismatch contrast in n-back. Left disabled.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise.
citations.quote: Trial-series RT fluctuations attributable to 1/f^alpha structure had within-subject SDs of roughly 15-40 ms in choice and memory tasks.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise.
citations.quote: Hurst exponents estimated from trial-series RTs in choice tasks cluster around 0.7-0.9, indicating long-range positive correlations.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f noise.
citations.quote: Spectral analyses of human RT series typically yield Hurst-equivalent exponents in the 0.65-0.85 range.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f^alpha (pink) RT structure with Hurst around 0.7-0.85 is characteristic of cognitive tasks including working memory (Gilden, 2001). sd_ms set to a moderate within-subject value.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: The n-back literature does not document a reliable conflict-adaptation-style lag-1 pair modulation analogous to congruency-sequence effects in conflict tasks.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Trial-pair (lag-1) interactions of preceding-trial type with current-trial RT were not reported as a reliable effect in this n-back validation study.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account.
citations.quote: Post-error trials are routinely excluded or treated separately when modeling sequential effects because the error event itself perturbs the next trial's RT.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: n-back literature does not document a reliable conflict-adaptation-style lag-1 pair modulation analogous to CSE in conflict tasks. Mechanism left disabled.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account.
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in choice tasks is on the order of 20-60 ms in healthy adults, dependent on error rate and ITI.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments.
citations.quote: Across speeded-choice and working-memory paradigms, post-error slowing magnitudes typically fall between ~20 and ~80 ms on the immediately following trial.

citations.authors: King, J. A., Korb, F. M., von Cramon, D. Y., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error behavioral adjustments are facilitated by activation and suppression of task-relevant and task-irrelevant information processing.
citations.quote: Working-memory tasks reliably elicit post-error slowing comparable in magnitude to that seen in simpler choice paradigms.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is documented in working-memory and n-back tasks (Notebaert et al., 2009; Steinhauser & Yeung, 2010). Magnitude in healthy adults is typically 30-70 ms on the immediately following trial.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SDs reflect typical healthy-adult variability in n-back (Kane et al., 2007; Jaeggi et al., 2010). Accuracy clip lower bound of 0.65 accommodates the harder 2-back match condition without admitting non-engaged participants; omission upper bound of 0.10 is consistent with attentive performance under a 1500 ms response window.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean RT in letter n-back tasks were on the order of 70-110 ms across conditions in young adults.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean RT in single n-back blocks ranged from ~60 to ~120 ms.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of the per-condition RT contrasts (1-back vs 2-back, match vs mismatch) were roughly half the SD of the overall RT mean.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence.
citations.quote: Individual sigma and tau estimates spanned roughly 0.6x to 1.4x the sample mean across young-adult participants in updating tasks.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of accuracy in single n-back conditions were typically 0.04-0.08 in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of n-back accuracy ranged from ~0.03 (1-back) to ~0.10 (2-back match) in young adults.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: Omission rates in single n-back blocks averaged ~2-4% in attentive young adults, with between-subject SDs around 0.01-0.02.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: Per-subject accuracy in 2-back match conditions ranged from ~0.6 to ~0.98 in young-adult samples after exclusion of non-engaged participants.

citations.authors: Kane, M. J., Conway, A. R. A., Miura, T. K., & Colflesh, G. J. H.
citations.title: Working memory, attention control, and the N-back task: A question of construct validity.
citations.quote: Participants below ~0.65 accuracy on 2-back conditions are typically flagged as non-engaged or chance-level.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure.
citations.quote: Omission rates above ~10% under a sub-1500 ms response window are commonly flagged as inattentive performance and excluded.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'n_back_rdoc' with 4 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 4 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects. Self-corrected after 1 schema-validation refinement(s).
Produced 42 citations across 28 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 33 parameters; 9 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 3 refinement(s): 68 trials matched, conditions ['match_1back', 'mismatch_1back'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
